description:
From the paradise islands of Trinidad and Tobago, to the Cricket lovers of the world, QUICKIT -a parody with dice by Parodice Games.

Experience the 'glorious uncertainties' of your favorite sport, with an exhilarating board game the whole family will enjoy.

Excellent ground, weather and light conditions all year round. At last, PLAY IS GUARANTEED.

A lovely day for Cricket, blue skies and gentle breeze…

View at: http://www.parodicegames.com/

With the 3rd player acting as referee, 2 players have 7 action cards and compete to select the correct one which matches one of 41 target cards, e.g. the letter on the postman, the flower for the lady. First to get it right takes the target card, get it wrong and you forfeit the target card. 
You win if you get the most target cards, but automatically lose if you play a dumb match, e.g. use the fire extinguisher on a person, fail to use the fire truck on the burning house. 
The ref deals the target cards and adjudicates, then plays the winner and so on. Rules in German and English. A good enough speed game, the sort of thing Reinhard Staupe could modernize nicely.

Published earlier under the name "Shock" with different pictures, but people had issues with playing "terrorist", "machine gun" and similar cards.
The Soundbytes device takes what you say, mixes it up, and plays it back. Then everyone tries to guess what in the world you were talking about.

boardgame: Travellers' Tour Through the United States
description:
The board is a map of the United States c. 1822, the westernmost states being Missouri and Louisiana. Because dice were frowned upon as instruments of the horrible vice of gambling, the game included a teetotum, a sort of spinner used to randomly yield a number from 1 to 8.  This is used to determine the first player. Lines are drawn on the map to connect cities in a continuous path which players must follow. The cities are not named, but numbered.

Upon arrival at a new city, the player must correctly name the place and, if playing the advanced version, its population, or lose a turn and try again.. The first to reach New Orleans (population 10,000) wins.

The first known game published in the United States. Unlike other games, it was also invented in the USA rather than being a copy of a British game, as many later ones were. The publisher were sellers of books in New York and later in 1822 also released Traveller's Tour Through Europe.

A copy is apparently still in existence and archived at the American Antiquarian Society.

boardgame: The Famous Monarchs of England Card Game
description:
The theme of this game is the kings and queens of England (and Oliver Cromwell), and is intended to be a more fun way to learn the names and dates of reign.

The goal is to collect sets of Monarchs in sequence, either in "date order" of reign or in "name & number order" (e.g., Henry 4, Henry 5, Henry 6) per the 2008 version rules.

The game play is very like ‘Rummy’ in style, using a card hand to build straight sequence sets.

1991 version contents:
57 portrait cards
4 reference cards
3 rules cards

2008 version contents are listed on contents card in image section below, and contains a few more player aid cards.

Detective Game for Juniors
Ages 5 - 7

Game Case
4 Game Cards
4 Movers
Notepad
family: Game: Cluedo / Clue (Parker Brothers, et al)
Game: Cluedo Junior / Clue Junior (Parker Brothers, et al)

description: The old king is eagerly awaiting his favorite daughter's impending wedding. You are one the finest goldsmiths in the land and must construct the finest wedding jewelry from rubies and sapphires and so on. There are 160 chips for the gems and 36 jewelry tiles.

Light trick-taking card game. Two separate decks, one for Oxford and the other for Cambridge, each with three suits of eight cards, plus three incident (wild) cards.

At the start of each hand, a player chooses the precedence of the suits. Only five tricks are played with each hand, this stops card counting and makes the game more luck-dependent.

Tricks count both for a boat (corresponding to the deck used) and for the player. Players that win a trick select which deck will be used and therefore which boat will win the point.

The first boat that scores 26 points wins. Players then score double for points won from the winning boat. Score is kept on an attractive pad with maps of the Boat Race course.

Hydra by Gilbert Levi is a tile-laying game with 60 square tiles.  Players take turns to draw and lay tiles, which show the snaking, dividing body or multiple heads of the Hydra. When a Hydra is totally completed, you score for the total number of heads, with a bonus point if there 3+ heads. Usual rules for tiles laying. Rather simple graphics, good for kids.

Think! This race game involves moving your coloured pawn along the track according to selections made with rotating discs, with some element of double-think. Not a dice game as the cover illustration shows. NOT to be confused with Denkste by Hexagames, which is a dice game.

Head or Button is an abstract game for 2 players in which the players' pieces are transparent. The board is a squared grid, the squares having 1,2 or 3 black dots or 4 colored dots which makes the pieces look like buttons. The game involves the pieces moving according to the numbers, individually or in combination.
Based on Alexander Pushkin's poem. Clone of "Talisman".

PRO-GRIDIRON is played by two opponents on a rectangular field (board) consisting of sixty-four rectangular, half light color and half dark color.  Each opponent has a team consisting of sixteen pieces.  All the pieces of this game have their own  value and power relative to the HEAD COACH.  With his authority and strategy, the HEAD COACH’S  ultimate desire is to beat the opponent’s HEAD COACH.   When a piece can force the HEAD COACH to move, this is called a Down.  If he stops the HEAD COACH’S movement,  this is a Touchdown.  The first team to get a Touchdown, therefore, wins the game.

Pick a category from the cards in your hand and name an item that belongs in that category. With every quick, correct answer, you throw away a card. Be the first player to get rid of all your cards and you win! It's that easy... with a few twists. It's fun... it's challenging... it's your time to RESPOND!

To start the game you arrange twelve UFO cards face up in a circle, with a Planet card inside it. UFO cards are divided in colors and have numbers on them.
The aim of the game is to play UFO cards from your hand in order to win the cards composing the circle. The cards you take score points.
A card played from the hand can be played only on the UFO card in the circle next to the Planet card. Playing a card gives you the possibility of moving the Planet card accordingly to the value of the card played.
UFO cards are played one at a time and the effect produced by each card is resolved before another card can be played (this gives you the possibility of building a strategy).
A card from the circle can be taken only by cards of the same color. Different colors simply take the place of the card on the table.
Simple math can be a valuable asset in sorting out a strategy.

A five-in-a-row game where each player has five pieces.    All the lines of five cross at the center piece, so it turns into a struggle for the control of the center of the board.  The pieces are 6-sided dice, and the up face determines how the piece can move.  When a piece is captured, it is rerolled and can enter play in its new form (i.e. it is reincarnated).  One of the pieces, the Buddah, is actually not played on the board, but instead fills a blank spot, acting as a wild card.

Based on information from the publisher's website:

Smart is about buying, selling and using Smarts in Smartcity. 'Eating' the Smarts determines your emotional situation on the Emotionhead.

In Smart players struggle to be the best Smartdealer and
to become the owner of three new Smartshops. To do this, players buy, sell and use Smarts in the twelve neighborhoods (gameboard).

In trading Smarts, you compare the popularity of different types of Smarts in the various neighborhoods, your distance from the neighborhoods in which there is demand for Smarts, the amount of various Smarts others want to  sell and the amount of Euro's others bid on new Smarts.

Trade is connected to the four actions in every round.
The Smarts you supply but don't sell are the Smarts you 'eat'. This 'eating' influences your emotional situation. How Relaxed, Dull, Hyper and Heavy you are, is indicated on the Emotionhead display. 
The corresponding actions can be used to improve your Smarttrade. So it's important to work on your emotional state.  Be careful of partial or even total epileptic attacks.

Available tactics include good bidding, good selling and good 'eating'. Doing well in one of these gives opportunities to do well in the other two. But sometimes it is important to 'take it easy'. In many cases the winner at the end doesn't sell in every round and doesn't play a large role in every auction. The best advice: play Smart!

Components:

     A2-sized city board, painted by Annette Wiesman
     real Smarts (actual candies, made of sugar, water soluble)
     real half walnut shells 
     play Euros 
     Emotionhead board

Standard fan trivia game about the Three Stooges. Players roll and move across the board, answering three types of questions: True/False, Multiple Choice, and Specific Answer. Players cannot move until they answer correctly; if three or more questions are missed, the player may declare themselves stuck, roll the die, and move backwards, in hopes of getting a different type of question. Questions range from the biographical ("When Moe Howard skipped school, where did he spend his free time?") to show-specific ("Which short features the phrase, 'Slowly I turn, step by step, inch by inch'?") Nostalgia will get you across the board, but only true fans will sweep the board.
Based on Sir Arthur Conan Doyle’s novel.
Based on the novel by Nikolai Nosov and on cartoon TV-series.
Based on the novels by Frank Baum and Alexander Volkov

boardgame: Sherlock Holmes Consulting Detective: Adventures by Gaslight
description:
"As the events unfold, day by day, can you clear Anny Forster of the charges against her?  Where are Amelie Thacker’s porcelain birds?  What is Mycroft’s role in this affair?  These are only a few of the mysteries that will challenge your deductive reasoning as you spend a few days in May of 1889 trying to solve the Adventures by Gaslight."

This is a supplement to the Sherlock Holmes Consulting Detective: The Thames Murders & Other Cases game and requires components from that game in order to play.  The game challenges your deductive reasoning skills and ability to discern important details from generally available information.  No luck is involved, just your mental ability.  Great fun with a group of fellow sleuths or as a challenge all by yourself.  Contents:

    Case Book -- a 24-page book containing the rules, daily briefings for each of the five days of the case, questions, solution and answers.
    Clue Book -- 143 numbered clues and the clue point listing.
    Newspaper Archive -- a collection of loose newspapers which provide clues and information pertaining to the cases as well as general Victoriana
    Newspaper and Post Card -- two of the clues you discover
    11" x 17" Map of Paris, circa 1889


It was originally published by Descartes Editeur in 1986 as the third volume for the French edition of the game as L'Affaire de L'Oiseau de Papier, and translated in 1988 into English, when it became the official fourth volume in the English Sherlock Holmes Consulting Detective series. The Queen's Park Affair, which is volume three in the English series, was volume four in France.

description:
A “what-if” scenario war game set in the year 2019 when Japan and the South American Union invade Mexico.  The Republic of Texas and the U.S. intervene.  It contains:

      1 20” × 30” full color playing map

The only board game with all the slime and grime of the music business.

Form a band, recruit a manager, secure a record contract, and then gig around the country in an attempt to get to Number One. You know, the old-fashioned way... before Pop Idol.

Chafing at his minor role in the Snit games, the god Runngus once again leaves Bulbous and his other companions and sets about creating his own game on his own world.

The game mainly consists of dropping counters from a three foot height, and hoping they land in auspicious areas of the map.

Published in Dragon magazine, Issue #40.
This light, racing game is probably the least known of all the Dirk Henn creations. Predict in secret who will win. Title literally means "dead end relationships."

Jasper Carrott is a popular Brummy comedian who has fronted TV shows using funny TV ads. His photos front this lame game in which players write "funny" captions to "whacky" photos from the vaults of some photo agency library. Guess who wrote which caption and score points. 200 photos and a huge waste. Oh look, a dog on a lawnmower. Call an ambulance, my sides have split with "much hilarity".

A Jenga/Mikado variant, de-construct a wooden pyramid without it falling and you losing, reconstruct a triangular structure and win. A surprisingly large wooden pyramid is made of 30 wooden pieces resting on a very solid wooden board, making the box really, really heavy.

Question - Who did Neely Capshaw file false sexual harassment charges against? Situation - A cafe on the beach names a delicious health shake after you. It becomes a best seller. Move forward two spaces.
Take on the role of one of the famous lifeguards in this marketing exercise and get some actual exercise. Roll & move round the beach, collecting discs, doing Physical Challenges (press-ups, star jumps), answering quiz questions or gamble on the situation, and then move on. Get three discs matching your character and win. With almost no publicity shots of the crew to embellish it, this game is so wholesome, it will make your teeth squeak.
Question - How many bottles of sunscreen do the Baywatch stars use in a week? Situation - A gang of rowdy youths tell you that their beers are non-alcoholic, they are fibbing of course. Move back one space for believing them.

Aliens Attack!Will you be strong enough to prevent their attack or will they consume the entire galaxy?
This game is a re-themed version of Pente with a small twist.

It consists of 80 counters (40 red and 40 silver) and an 8X8 battle grid that represent space.
Each player takes 40 counters.
One player represents the Aliens that are attacking the galaxy and the other is the defending force.
There are several starting positions that players can choose from in order to start a game.
Each starting possition presents players different chalenges.
The goal of this game is to manage to control the enemy territory and try to avoid losing your base.
Players add counters in turns to the board in order to block enemy counters and create lines of their counters.
Once a counters has been put to the board it can not be moved.
The aim is to align counters of the same colour in vertical hirizontal or diagonal lines.In order to capture your opponents counters you must flank in pairs your opponents counters in any same direction.
The winner is the player who at the end of the game has captured more enemy counters or the player who has captured 5 enemy counters in a row.

Hoodwinked is a children's game with a mix of puzzles and challenges that the player must complete while the world is upside-down. You wear a pair of goggles with a mirror pointing down at 45 degrees, so your view of the world is inverted. The game has a deck of cards showing tasks, such as word puzzles, drawing puzzles and building with little wooden blocks. The quarter-board shows a crazy room and you progress from task to task until you reach to the finish.

This is a seven card expansion for Beutelschneider.

It adds cards that can be played in a trick instead of cards dealt to affect the current trick.  The cards are used only once and then discarded.

After the Little League game the players participate in a brawl.  You control cards representing the different kids who have different skills who then fight it out with hands and weapons.  Last player standing wins.

boardgame: Crisis: A Twisted Laugh at Life
description:
From the publisher's website:

Your worst nightmares have come true. We've taken your life, packaged it, and are selling it back to you... as a board game! That's right, it's just that easy. Kicked out of the house by your parents, you must now learn to cope. Will your reputation be good? You'll have to maintain employment, avoid life's temptations and quite possibly go to school. Or will you prove to be bad? Crime sprees, excessive gambling, and weaseling the one's who love you most. Either way there's no way of avoiding Crisis™. Survival is the key. Sound easy? Think again. Mr. X (that's you!) is a loser!

(The game appears to be one of roll, move, draw a card and apply it.)

description: In this game, players compete to build the largest wall in Ancient China. Choosing from a hand of possible structures, players take turns adding sections to their walls, forming vast, snaking barriers to surround territory and gain military might. A cunning sense of strategy and a small bit of luck combine to decide the next emperor of China.

This is a representative of this peculiarly American class of board game produced for political reasons, as a form of parody.  For some reason, most instances of these games come from the right wing (another game in the same class is Capital Punishment, which is much more vitriolic in its political commentary).

The "object" of this game is to "go broke and go on Welfare with the help of government grants and boondoggles".  As the player tokens go around the board, their starting money eventually dwindles to nothing, at which point they no longer receive nor pay out money (ignoring the instructions on most of the board spaces) and exclusively draw from the Welfare fund (a separate pile of money) each turn they go around the board.  The game continues until the Welfare is exhausted, and the winner is then the player who got the most Welfare.
Generation of an ideal girl.
Based on the novel by Jonathan Swift.

This expansion to Kardinal & König allows the game to be played by two players.  It adds a third, neutral, player (the King) which players alternate playing pieces of onto the board.  Five new influence cards indicate for which player the King's pieces score.  Additionally, each player has three special action cards which allow them to break certain rules when surrendered.
