From the Italian company International Team comes this fairly simple game of piracy on the high seas.

On a map that can only be considered a fantasy world you sail your galleon around the outer islands picking up treasure chests in the hope of delivering the treasures to his island in the middle of the board.

Movement is handled by way of a wind disk. Every 3 turns this disk turns and the wind will be coming from a new direction. A ship always moves in a straight line that can be at a diagonal. If it is sailing in the direction of the wind it must move 4 spaces. If in a diagonal with the wind it must move 5 spaces. If perpendicular to the wind movement is 3 spaces. If diagonal against the wind movement is 2 spaces and if against the wind 1 space. The wind rotates in a rigid sequence so you can always plan what your ship will be doing. If a ship runs onto land it is sunk. A ship can stop short of its full movement if it is in a harbor square of which each island has one. On the outer islands this is where a galleon picks up treasures and each may carry up to 3. The board wraps around on itself so if a ship sails off an edge it reappears on the opposite side of the board.

There are 6 dark areas on the seas that can become storms. The start player rolls 2 die at the beginning of his turn and puts a marker on the corresponding areas. Ships cannot enter these and ships that are in these areas cannot move.

Each player also has a frigate. Frigates attempt to pass over another ship, regardless of the type, which will sink it. A sunken ship will always reappear at that player’s island at the start of his next turn. Frigates may not dock at harbors so they cannot end their movement short when passing over one. Ships sunk have their treasures returned to the bank.

You play until a player has collected an agreed to amount of treasures. The rules suggest 8 to 20.

The rules take up 3 small pages of which half the page is rules in another language. There are also 4 illustrations in the rules so you end up with about 1 page of rules. Some things are very vague though. How the Disk of Winds is used is not very clear. Here logic can get you through. The islands in the center are not marked as belonging to anyone so I have always had each player put a treasure on one to claim it. We have also played that the islands belong to no one player and you just need to get you treasures to an island. This makes it a lot easier to dock and drop off treasures.

Quad Checkers is an abstract strategy game where 4 players move small discs on an 8x8 board, attempting to "capture" an opponent's pieces. It is very similar to the basic Checkers game.

On each turn, players may only move in a diagonal direction. If a player's piece cannot move to a square, as an opponent's piece is occupying it, the piece is instead moved to the following diagonal square and the opponent's piece is removed from the board (captured). If the following space has another piece in it, the aforementioned move cannot be made. If, in capturing a piece, you are in a position to capture another piece, you may do so until no further pieces can be captured. This is the only time multiple moves may be taken per turn.

Quad Checkers differs from the original game in that, adjacent to each edge of the board, is an additional 8x3 grid. This is the starting point of the playing pieces for each of the 4 players. From here, pieces move out into the main grid where the competitive play takes place.

The game is lost for a player if he/she cannot move or if all of his/her pieces are eliminated from the board.

A game where the object is to build 3 cars on your assembly line. You start out with just the Chassis and must move along the assembly line to acquire the Wheel Base, Engine Block, Steering Posts, and Body to complete each vehicle. The first person to complete all 3 car assemblies is the winner. The car companies represented in the game are Plymouth, Studebaker, Chevy, and Ford.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description: An amalgam of Scattergories and Spoons.  Players are given four cards, and must pass one and draw one until someone has a four-letter word that fits the category drawn at the beginning of the round.  When someone grabs a beanbag signaling he or she has a word, everyone grabs for one.    When someone is caught six times without a bag, the game ends, and whomever has the most points wins the game.

The object of Bioscopy is to be the first player or team to score 20 points in each of the 4 categories of trivia (Who, What, When, and Where).

Each player or team puts their playing marker at the center of the board, and their 4 scoring markers on the 0 at the start of each of the 4 scoring scrolls. Players take turns rolling a six-sided die to determine how far you may move on the board, moving in any direction they choose (but they may not visit the same space twice in a turn). The spaces on the board are color coded to each of the 4 categories of questions, and are marked with a number (10, 7, 4, and 1) that corresponds to the difficulty of the clue. Each card has 4 clues on it, descending in value (10, 7, 4, and 1). Players may hear the clues in descending value until they reach the minimum value indicated on their space on the board -- if they can successfully answer at a higher point clue value, they will receive the higher points.

An alternate form of scoring is based on stealing points from the opponents by landing on a space on the board without a number. When this happens, the player competes head-to-head with the person that has scored the most points on that category's scoring scroll.

Royal Steeple Chase is a very astonishing game, published in 1939 by Miro Company in France.
4 horses compete on a track of 21 spaces, with hedges and rivers. The players receive cards which indicate two actions. They use these to bid on the horse of their choice.  They can then buy better cards at auction. The money from the bids is shared equally between all the players, and then the race takes place. Each player in turn plays a card and carries out its two actions in the preferred order. The horses which win and place second award points to their owners. If no horse finishes, the players who did not have a horse gain points.
The game contains several variants, some of which were inspired by Bridge and were created by French officers who were prisoners of war in Germany in 1941.

Royal Steeple Chase est un jeu très etonnant, paru en 1939 chez Miro Company en France.
4 chevaux s'affrontent sur une piste de 21 cases, avec des haies et des rivières. Les joueurs reçoivent des cartes qui indiquent deux actions. Ils enchérissent ensuite pour acheter le cheval de leur choix. Ils peuvent ensuite acheter de meilleures cartes aux enchères. Les mises sont partagées équitablement entre tous les joueurs. Puis la course a lieu. Chacun à son tour pose une carte et réalise ses deux actions dans l'ordre de son choix. Le cheval qui gagne et le second cheval rapportent des points à leur propriétaire. Si aucun cheval n'arrive, les joueurs qui n'avaient pas de cheval gagnent des points.
Le jeu contient plusieurs variantes. Plusieurs variantes intégrées dans le jeu sont inspirées du Bridge et ont été créées par des officiers prisonniers de guerre en Allemagne en 1941.

Tochtli is a strategy game in which you out-maneuver your opponent by moving, jumping, splitting, or stacking your game pieces to control one of six triangles.

Components:

     One game board consisting of 12 spaces forming 6 outer points (triangles).
     Six pieces of each color (12 total)

The gameboard is a 7x9 grid. The players draw from a bag containing seventy-eight tiles called nuggets. Each nugget represents one of six metals. Each player, in turn, places one, two or three nuggets on the gameboard. The nuggets placed must match each other, however. After placement of the nuggets, the player may purchase up to two shares of stock of the mines represented by the nuggets on the gameboard. The player then ends the turn by replacing the nuggets played with nuggets from the bag. Every subsequent turn has each player beginning the turn by collecting profit based on the number of shares owned and the size of each mine and rarity of the metal therein.

The game ends when the gameboard is filled. All players cash out the value of their stocks and the player with the most money is declared the victor.

Players are dealt a hand of card and attempt to form the highest scoring equation in each of the four rounds -- addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division. For example, a player holding the cards 0, 0, 4, 4, 6, 6, 6, 8, 8, 9 could form this equation during the addition round and score 864:

800 + 64 = 864

Using every card is not mandatory. During a round, players pass their hands, so each player plays every hand once, eliminating the luck of the draw.

"Challenging Game of Creative Equations"

Belongs to the 3M Gamette Series.

Crypt: The Pharaoh's Curse is characterized as a "Trading Board Game", available in Starter Box Sets of 5 hexes (hexagonal tiles), 3 six-sided dice, 3 character cards, 34 action pieces (cards), and 20 life/creature tokens. Booster sets are also available.

Each player selects a character card secretly before the beginning of the game, which will affect which action pieces that the player will be most effective with, determines the minimum and maximum number of action pieces that the player must play with, and describes any special abilities of the character. Next, each player chooses which action pieces they will play with from their collection, subject to the limits of the character card. Finally, each player contributes an agreed-upon number of hexes (rooms) to the game in their own stack.

Gameplay consists of drawing/discarding action pieces,  playing any number of free action pieces, taking one action (such as moving, exploring by adding a new hex to the board and resolving the occurrence on the hex, playing an action piece, attacking another player/creature, or exiting the crypt).

The goal is to be the first player to find your key in the action piece deck, find and place your exit tile, and successfully exit the crypt complex.

Demonwand represents a struggle between powerful wizards for control of a magical land. The only human characters in the game are the Wizards themselves and the greatest warriors; ordinary mortals stay out of the way when dragons battle with demons and giants. The Wizards attempt to rally neutral beings to their cause, and to hamper their opponent's efforts by moving the very terrain itself. The landscape is enchanted, and its mountains and forests are subject to powerful spells. With these spells, the magic portals called Gates, and his own forces, each Wizard hopes to give his rivals their death blows. The winner is the last Wizard left in the land.

Published in Fantasy Gamer #5.

boardgame: Lost Worlds Adventure Set I: In the Ruins of the Ancients
description:
This is the first boardgame based on the Lost Worlds series of fighting gamebooks. Players would take miniatures into a 3-D plastic molded dungeon room, and maneuver on the board until they came into melee range, at which point they switched to the Lost Worlds books to resolve combat.

Rules were provided for movement, ranged combat and furniture. Several scenarios came with the game.

Part of the Lost Worlds series.
family: Series: Picture Book Games (Nova Game Designs)

description:
A thematic series in the Lost Worlds series (these books are even compatible with the others). Players each take a character represented with a game book and a stat sheet. By giving the book to the opposite player, the pages give a first person view to the combatants. As various moves direct the players to turn to this page or that, combat damage, restrictions, and other special events are relayed to the players via the pages of the book.

Part of the Lost Worlds series of books.
family: Series: Picture Book Games (Nova Game Designs)

description: Karl May Spiel is a game rather intended for the children. The principle is classic. The players are in confrontation in a running where it is necessary to be the first one in the arrival, having found two identical objects and having crossed different obstacles. The originality of the game lives in the die. In fact, there is no die, because the players determine their advance by a shot to the elastic representing archery. It is thus the fitness and not the fate that leads the game, but for some players, it is about the same thing!

It takes money to make money. Each player will be putting up capital in anticipation of Profits. If you want the Big Bucks, though, you will have to form a Corporation with other players. A Corporation will net you Corporate Rewards if everyone cooperates -- but if any members defect (or if an outsider tries to horn in on the action) it's back to the drawing board.

Cassa Grande is a Roland Siegers's small and fun game. The idea is very simple, rules are read in two minutes and a game need 10 minutes. The players have the same set of cards at the beginning of the play. Each at his turn plays a card back to front on the table in a virtual tray of 6x6. When all the cards are put, you turn the whole and each books its points. Cards form numbers which are differently read as we are in the North, the South, the East or the West.

Die Drei, means the three games. The box indeed contains the material and the German rules to play three Alex Randolph's games: Hepta, Pegs, Tetrods. It also contains the rules of T. for two, a game created by Franz-Josef Schulte with the pieces of Tetrods.

Hepta is also known as Hepta (Ravensburger) or Magic 7 (Ideal).
Pegs was published as Peggino (Ravensburger).
Tetrods was never published before.
T. for two was never published before.

The VANITPL8 Game takes the English language and pushes the phrase"creative license" to the limit. Up to 3 clues will help you unscramble these challenging vanity license plates.

Players race against each other and a hour glass to unscramble phonetically written phrases.  For example, 8ATVILNP unscrambled is VANITPL8, which says vanity plate.

Very fun and challenging for the whole family.  Available at http://www.vanityplategame.com/

Life as a Blackman is a game with a political message: It's harder than you think to be young and black. Players all start as 18-year-old black males either in Glamourwood, Black University, the Military, or in the Ghetto. Through die rolls, players work their way through the randomly selected starting areas and into Downtown, an approximation of life in the real world. Players may find themselves going to Church to right past wrongs, or spend time in Prison for making bad decisions. The first person to reach the Freedom space at the top of the board wins.

This expansion for Castle of Magic contains a reprint of the first expansion and additional material in the form of three new scenarios.
   Material from the first expansion has been generalized to use with all scenarios.
   The expansion consists of a single perfect-bound book. Almost all the pages must be torn out and copied. In some instances this means copying it onto cardstock and creating decks of cards.

Expands:

    Castle of Magic

Set up surveillance cameras to blackmail opponents, send them blackmail letters, receive wage increments or reductions, hire thugs and private investigators or even let the FBI in on someone's blackmail scam.

W.A.C.K.O. stands for Wild and Crazy Kids Only.

These series of action/dexterity games included the following:
BMX Racer
Gobblers
Shoot the Rapids
Escape from Devil's Castle
Baseball
Alligator Alley
Hoops
Jungle Safari

Some were just races, with each person taking a turn and the best (fastest, most completions) would win, while others actually had a head to head component like Hoops (basketball) or Gobbler (like hungry-hungry hippos).
family: Admin: Outside the Scope of BGG

description: Round Four is a variant of Connect Four. 2-4 players can play at the same time. The object is still to match four of your color chips in a straight line. The board is four circles inside of each other. the three outer most circles have 16 holes for pieces while the inner circle only has eight. Also some of the holes can fit two pieces inside of it, one on top the other. An added depth to this game is that each circle can be rotated clockwise. During your turn, you can either place a piece, or rotate one of the rings clockwise, causing all of the pieces to shift on that circle. The winner has four of his color pieces in a straight line starting from the inner circle and moving out.

boardgame: Renegades:  Eldar and Chaos Armies for Space Marine
description:
Renegades:  Eldar and Chaos Armies for Space Marine, does not come with any extra figures for playing Space Marine, but comes with rules and background information for Eldar and Chaos armies.  It also contains a set of Eldar and Chaos epic army cards for both the Eldar and Chaos army to add to the basic Space Marine game.

Expands:


    Space Marine

Dark Lore: An Epic Fantasy Adventure Game for Your Mind, is a Sci-Fi and Horror Movie, Supernatural, Monsters, Magic, Superstition, Mixed Macabre, Comedy Horror trivia game. In it you role a die, move your piece a certain number of Spaces and answer a trivia question. The subject is based on the number on the die you role. There are spaces that send you back some on the board. Also there are some spaces that you receive cards that can move you ahead, behind or move another player forwards are backwards. There are over a thousand questions. Some sample questions that appear on the back of the box are:

What is Agent Mulder's nickname on the "X-Files"?
What color cat is bad luck if it crosses your path?
Who is the father of Hercules?
What must a weapon be made from to kill a werewolf?
What must you do to a vampire after you drive a stake through its heart?
Where does a Jedi Knight get his mystical power?

Play if You Dare

Gundam M.S.War Trading Card Game is based on the popular Japanese anime series based on giant robots.  Gundam first started in 1979 and continues to this day with various series, movies, models, and toys and now a collectible card game.  The particular gundam series it is based on is Gundam Wing, although they were going to release other expansions to add more series to the game.

To play the game, you need two opposed decks. In this case you need the Wing Gundam starter Set and the Oz Corps Starter Decks.  You play cards depicting giant robots and can equip them with character cards to increase the attack strength.  The  cards are played by playing their cost points.  The cost if discarding a certain number of cards.  Then the robots battle and the loser loses a certain number of cards from his draw pile, when the draw pile is empty, the game is over.

description: This is a pretty esoteric game. The object of the game is to rescue POW's from Viet Nam's infamous Hanoi Hilton. The first player to rescue and escape with a POW from the camp cell and reach the end of the rescue/escape route wins the game. The designer believed that POW's were still being held in North Viet Nam and apparently created this game to keep people aware of that fact. He comments " POW Rescue is an action game of rescue dedicated to those still held as POW with the hope that Americans will never forget those we left behind". The rules for this very simplistic game are 3 very short pages in length.

Australia became a federation on the 1st of January 1901. So our history books tell us. The colonies came together, united into one nation. Not by an act of war, nor through violent action was a nation forged. How boring! No wonder I fell asleep in those Australian history classes.

This is your chance to rewrite a nations history. Federation had failed. Instead of peaceful unity, war between the colonies is declared. All agreements between the colonies have been dissolved.

It is Spring 1901. The divisions between the colonies is fueled by a hunger to gain control over the continent. You are the colonies  governor and have already instructed the militia forces to be mustered and ready for action.

Who will succeed to unify Australia? Who will forever be doomed to oblivion? What will you do to unite Australia?

Federation Diplomacy is a variant of the popular Hasbro game called Diplomacy. Were as Diplomacy is based in Europe with the great powers at the turn of the 20th century, Federation Diplomacy is set in Australasia in the same time. It requires a keen eye for tactics and the silver tongue of the diplomat to weave a web of alliances to see your selected colony dominate the board.

It is played mainly by email with movement adjudicated by an electronic judge. For further information please refer to the web site:

http://members.austarmetro.com.au/~theok/dip/federation/home.html

A book containing three games:


Pentagon

Pentagon is not a simulation in the formal sense of the word. The events in the game are intended to be illustrative and the decisions fictitious, woven from the progress of play. But by giving the players incentives both to compete and to cooperate, the game does simulate bureaucratic politics. And the content of bureaucratic politics is similar in tone and substance to real debates over military budgets. Pentagon shows how political factors influence the shape of budgets before the bureaucracy even gets down to decisions on weapons procurement and their merits.


The R&D Game: Congressional Chutes and Ladders

The R&D Game is designed as a solitaire game. It is based on a decision-tree mechanism that leads the player through a set of alternative developments. This type of approach is sometimes called a "scripted role" model because the main emphasis is on broad decision choices rather than narrow selection of tactics. Given the broad choice, the decision tree itself generates much of the action, unlike a two-player or multi-player game in which action flows from the decisions of each of the players.


The Last Days of Saigon

An introductory level wargame.  The goal of each player is to destroy the other player's forces, so the game does not follow the historical values.  The map is pretty basic, moderately large scale, 3-color with just basic terrain. Units are mostly Brigade-level units with a few regimental and batallion units thrown in. There is a mostly abstract representation of air, and naval units. [Note: This game was also published as 'Last Days At Saigon' in 'Bridge' magazine, Volume 4 #1 (11/75) bound-in, with b&w components, as part of a 'Vietnam in Retrospect' issue.]

description:
Ronin is a customizable miniatures game of gigantic mechanized battle. In Ronin you design your own unique battle armor and equip it with heavy turbo rocket launchers, jump packs and assault cannons of your choice. Make tactical decisions on what energy-fluids to use and how to process them through your system in order to cool weapon systems or boost your movement circuits.

Design your Ronin from the 102 different parts of weaponry and equipment, and numerous different Ronin designs. All parts are interchangeable.

- 60 mm scale vouch for high detail and great presence on the tabletop.

- Fast and intense combat including missile weapons and close combat covering hit locations and critical hits.

- Features the unique MAG-HIT magnet indication system.

Expanded by:

    Ronin: War

A lighthearted cow-tipping game in which aliens are trying to tip cows for bragging rights.



In the first part of the game, players place their cows. 
On an 8x8 grid (3 players) or 9x9 grid (2 or 4 players), players take turns placing 12 cow chits of their color, 1 at a time and 1 per cell, inside the fence, which is a 1-cell perimeter. When placing cows, players try to achieve Cow Lock, which is two identically-colored orthogonally adjacent cows. Four cows in a square is called a Bovine Doom Block. Next, each player places their alien chit on the center square of their edge of the board (on the grid, but outside the fence). They take turns moving the alien up to 3 spaces, and then they may choose to tip cows. Tipping starts from the alien and stops at a Cow Lock -- the alien cannot tip a cow directly into a cow of the same color. The player then collects all tipped cows.

To determine individual scores, each player tallies their cows, subtracting the number of cows of their color from the remaining cows. High score wins.

In the 2-player game, each player uses a single alien marker as normal, but chooses and places cows of 2 colors. Cow Lock and Bovine Doom Block can still only be achieved with cows of the same color.


From the packaging:
In Udder Madness you are an alien engaged in a contest with your alien pals to see who can tip the most cows of your opponents while tipping as few of your own as possible.

Why an alien, you ask? Well there are a couple of reasons.

One reason is that by now, any alien visitors we may have must surely have grown bored with abducting people and examining their nether-regions. So with nether-regions out, and crop circles quickly becoming pass, they're going to need some new mystifying activity... Why not this?

The second reason is that we at Placebo Press could not possibly believe that an actual human being, no matter how depraved or intoxicated, would participate in such an activity outside of the confines of a harmless board game? Hence it must be aliens. I don't think that our logic is flawed here.

[for more information on this game and to play online, visit www.allumbra.com]

Lost in the ancient Celtic migration across Europe, this game was played between the clan chieftains to resolve disputes peacefully rather than resort to a costly and bloody war. It was played on a swatch of ornamental cloth familiar to this day as a "plaid". Instead of stones used in the traditional game, this variation presented here uses dice as the playing pieces.

To win at Breacan - and thus resolve the dispute in your favor, you must be the first to weave a path from one side of the Breacan to the opposite side.

Placing the dice
On your turn, place two of your four rolled dice. After your turn has ended, two more dice will be automatically rolled to replace the two you rolled.

Dice can be placed in two ways:
1. They can be placed on a square of your own color (e.g. red on red) when that square has no vertical or horizontal neighbors.
2. They can be placed on any square as long as each adjacent (horizontally or vertically) neighboring die of your color is either one higher or one lower than the die being placed. Note: 6 can be placed next to a 1 and 1 next to a 6.

Winning
The first player to complete a path from top to bottom or from side to side is the winner.

boardgame: Le Tour du Monde en 80 Jours
description:
Each player steers two people, FOGG and PASSEPARTOUT in a journey Around the World in 80 Days, represented by 80 compartments. To move, players have tickets with two numbers. The first number is for FOGG and the other one is for his servant. All the players possess the same equipment. The purpose is not to finish first, but to make both of your travelers arrive in certain compartments at the same time. It is thus necessary to manage your tickets in a rational way, and naturally, the opponents have the same goal. The place is limited on compartments !


Chaque joueur dirige deux personnages, FOGG et PASSEPARTOUT dans un tour du monde en 80 jours, représentées par 80 cases. Pour avancer, ils disposent de tickets comportant d'un coté le nombre de cases pour FOGG et de l'autre le nombre de cases de son serviteur. Tous les joueurs possèdent le même équipement. Le but n'est aucunement d'arriver le premier, mais de faire arriver les deux voyageurs sur des cases particulières en même temps. Il faut donc gérer ses tickets de manière rationnelle, et bien entendu, les adversaires ont le même but. Pour corser le tout, la place est limitée sur les cases.

Players write their 5 best answers to questions on cards... i.e. 'Name 5 fictional characters you would like to invite to dinner.' Players then compare answers and tally matches and non-matches. There are 15 questions per game. The player with the lowest difference in matches and non-matches though the fun of the game is mostly found in the interesting conversations and debates that always follow each question.

[For more information and to play online visit www.allumbra.com]

Metis is a pure strategy game in which you guide your Priests in exchanging power with each other to ultimately wrest control of your opponent’s Temple of Metis.

Your Priests all have varying degrees of power. Those with greater power can make sweeping movements greatly altering the course of the struggle. Lesser Priests, while more limited in might, are able to move more freely.

Movement

A piece can move horizontally, vertically or diagonally exactly the number of spaces as the value currently on the die. After, you move, transfer power with the pieces you jumped (see below).

Landing on Pieces: You may land on a piece (yours or your opponent’s) only if your piece has a higher value than the one it is landing on. The piece that you land on is destroyed. The capturing piece gains +1 power.

Power Transfer

As pieces move over one another, they exchange power. You give (1) power from your moving piece to each of your own pieces that you jump. Your piece takes (1) power from each enemy piece it passes over.

Underpowered and Overpowered Pieces: During movement, if a piece falls below (1) power, it is has become underpowered and is removed from the board. Likewise, if a piece gains more than (6) power, it has become overpowered and is removed from the board.

Winning

There are two possible ways to win Metis.

1. Occupy seats in your opponents Temple of Metis. There are two areas in each players temple: The inner chamber (darker) and the outer chamber (lighter). Occupying a seat in the inner chamber is worth 2 occupation points. occupying a seat in the outer chamber is worth 1 occupation point. You must hold 8 occupation points to win the game.

2. Destroy opponent’s pieces until she has three or less (making the above goal impossible for your opponent).

Similar to Battleship, but with more complicated rules.

Each player deploys ships in secret on their copy of the mapsheet. Then they try to find all of the enemy ships and destroy them.

The basic rules allow for simple movement and firing. The advanced rules cover submerging of submarines, reload time, torpedoes, and depth charges.

Ship movement is always announced in terms of direction but not location.

Two different mapsheets are included.  One for basic play and another for advanced.

Rosetta is a tile laying game where players are trying to match the designs on the tiles to form a complete picture.
The two main game types are co-op in that players are all working together to complete the design. It can be thought of as a puzzle, with square pieces, that has multiple solutions.

On one side of the tiles, the "Rosetta", 1-3 players work together to make a 6x6 square picture. When complete, the picture shows different coloured flowers, each made up of two corresponding square pieces. You may choose to use the 4 joker tiles as wild cards.

On the other side, "Puncola", you are creating a 6x6 square picture by matching the edges of the tiles with a similar pattern on an adjacent tile. At the corners of certain tiles is a 1/4 circle that is paired with 3 other tiles to form a complete circle.

"Domino-Puncola" is an alternate version of the game that adds competition. Each player picks 5 tiles at random and, in turn, tries to match an edge of the "Puncola" side to a tile on the table. If that person cannot place his/her tile, he/she draws a new one. The first person to place all of his/her tiles is the winner.

Welcome to the land of sunshine, palm trees and yep, you guessed it, those oh so cool California Raisins.  Raisins are coming to Hollywood from all over California to be singing and dancing stars.  In fact 'conga' lines of raisins can be seen all over the city.  But which raisins will hit the 'big time'?  It's all timing in Hollywood.  Be the first player to get your quartet on center stage and your four raisins will be the 'stars' of the show.

according the the Making of The California Raisins Board Game, three artists spent more than three months to design, create and perfect the three-dimensional sculpted clay model that was photographed to make the game board.

Everyone knows Garfield loves to eat lasagna, play with Pooky, sharpen his claws on Jon's favorite chair, and tease Odie. Be the first to collect 4 cards of any one of his favorite things, and Garfield says you're the winner!

WORLD 7 the Help! Where's the Rest of the World?! game plays with the fun and ease of the old-fashioned rummy-type card games--but, there is much more than rummy in this fast "Trip of Cards" across the 7 continents of the world.  Players race to be the first to connect all their cards in a continuous chain of connecting countries.  But, WATCH OUT!  There are many "kickers" along the way!
Can be played as singles or partners.
Parents' Choice Foundation Award Winning Game!
By the makers of USA Trip 50.

Bulls, Bears and Billionaires is a board game that makes winner a Millionaire on a short game or a Billionaire for a long game.  It takes making money and turns it into even more fun because you are buying and selling stocks as the prices go up and down.  And to help you relieve your stress, you get to yell out your stock orders and when you sell your stock you must throw the trading tickets on the floor.  The Bulls, Bears and Billionaires game allows you have fun while learn more about the real stock market.

Players roll dice and that causes the stock prices to change, sometimes just one stock price changes; other rolls cause all stocks prices to go up or down.  It’s a game that test wit against daring.  Should you sell out now or wait for a higher price.  Or, if your stock price is down, double up the price by using the Rumor Mill but beware—stock prices can go down just as fast as they go up.  The game uses Good News and Bad News to change stock prices just like the real stock market.  There is even an “Insider Trading Violation” penalty to keep all things equal.

All stock prices go up with a Bull Market and watch out for the Bear Markets when they all go down.  Rolling doubles takes on a whole new meaning in this game.   One double reduces your stock shares while another doubles your holdings.  The game is a fast pace of buying and selling stocks.  It creates an atmosphere of constant participation because every move affects any and all players.  It doesn’t take long to learn strategies to help you win.  Build an alliance with other traders to try and control the market or force the prices down on stocks you do not own.  It is a great game for the family or those more serious players looking for a real challenge.

Hex-and-miniature game in which players fly spaceships filled with lemmings around the game area hoping to intentionally kill off their entire crew as quickly as possible.  The trick is, upgrading your ship and exploration (which may lead to successes rather than failures) are the best ways to approach your doom faster.  As your ship grows more powerful, you make a better target, but you'll do more damage to other players' ships.  The objective is to lose all 28 members of your crew.

Index Pro Football 2002 is the new, solitaire NFL Statistical Simulation Game designed for NFL Fans who loves to keep stats of their favorite NFL Players while replaying NFL Games of the previous 2001 NFL Season.......The play-by-play recording onto the Game Logs and the huge dose of statistical accuracy brings gameplay excitement.......This game is a statistician's dream !  http://www.indexprofootball.com

An asymmetrical board game in which one player plays a nasty monster while the other player(s) take on the roles of family members defending their home from the evil intruder.  If the monster can kill the whole family, he wins.  If the family gets the monster first, they win.  The monster has certain advantages, special abilities, and is unidentified until he is seen by a family member, but family members can utilize items around the house (like moose-heads, ties, and remote-control cars) to attack the much stronger monster.  The game uses scalable rules ranging from basic (Go Cower Under Your Blankie While Crying For Your Mommy), Intermediate (fearfully challenging) which adds in monster powers and more complex combat rules, and Advanced (Hero Worship) which adds in even more combat rules, monster abilities, and allows one or all family members to be significantly stronger than the rest.

Game equipment consists of 72 letter tiles (color-coded to indicate vowel, common consonant, or less-common consonant) and two frames. Each frame holds 19 letter tiles in an array of four interlocking words, of 4, 5, 6 and 7 letters; the frames can be rotated and flipped to form 16 different patterns.  A player draws 19 to 25 tiles (depending on desired level of difficulty), balancing the colors to get a good mix of letters, and tries to form words in the frame.  Can be played solitaire or competitively.

Fluxx meets Wyatt Earp.  Win by being the first to get three chips which match the three face-up "Wanted" posters.  But the cards played can not only let other players take your chips, they can require you to turn your chips over (displaying a different outlaw on the reverse) or can replace one Wanted poster with a new one.

Players are shown a picture of a "crime scene" and try to answer questions about it.  Since only twelve pictures come with the game, repeated playability is limited.

While there is only one listing for Crime Busters, it should be noted that three volumes were released, each with 12 mysteries to solve.

A card displaying two or three letters is drawn, and players list as many words beginning with those letters as they can within the time limit.  Points are awarded for number of words, number of words not found by other players, and length of words.
Rummy-like game in which players try to get rid of their cards by laying down "systems" consisting of U.S. cities and the (historic) railroads connecting them.
Players advance around a track by guessing words with double meanings (A vagabond/To walk with heavy feet = tramp) or homonyms (Metal rooftop rooster/Mirror watcher = vane/vain).

Players give crossword-style clues for words that can be placed on the board.  A four-player partnership game, with two- and three-player variants.

Knock-on-Word! is sort of a redesign of Joli Kansil's own Montage.

Deep inside his crystal castle the mysterious King Laurin prevails since legendary times. His subjects are dwarves, who dig treasures from rock, in order to fill his vaults. In the wonderful garden of the king the dwarves show their lord the most precious treasures to rise in his favor. But only few will succeed in acquiring liberty ....

Rummy game with a 75-card deck, consisting of: the 52 standard cards; 13 "quadrate" cards bearing a rank and all four suits, with the effective suit determined by the roll of a special die; and ten wild cards.

Game for 2 to 4 players which combines strategy with a setting of medieval war and intrigue among the Scottish clans.

You are a Clan Chief trying for the regency of Scotland. You can attack other peoples pieces, & depending on whose territory you attack them in they could be injured, captured or killed. Along with attempting to gain the crown, and sabotaging your rivals attempts, you must also try not to get cursed by the witch, for then your lineage would end with your current Heir.
A motorized "Disc-Dealer" releases 40 shaped discs two at a time which players attempt to match up to the 9 shapes in their board quadrant.  The first player to match all 9 shapes wins.

This expansion contains everything you need to include two additional players in The Starfarers of Catan, the exciting, fast moving, science fiction game.  Here you'll find 2 mother ships, 2 sets of playing pieces, additional cards, 2 reusable gameboard overlays, and a new alien race ripe for encounter.

Now you can play one of five or six Starfarers commissioned to explore the galaxy, build new colonies, and expand commercial ties with the various races of the Galactic Council.  Accept the challenge!  Careful diplomacy, clever trade, a firm stance against pirates, and the bold colonization of distant planetary systems will win you the support of humanity and the other races of the galaxy and make you the Council's Galactic Ambassador!

Expands:

     The Starfarers of Catan


Belongs to the Catan Series.

description:
Now up to six players can muster their knights against the scourge threatening Catan!  The 5-6 Player Expansion for The Cities and Knights of Catan allows you to expand and inject more excitement into your games without sacrificing ease of play.  Designed for five or six players, it adds even more drama to the award-winning game of culture, politics, and warfare.

Belongs to the Catan Series.

The 5-6 Player Expansion for The Seafarers of Catan allows you to add 1-2 more opponents without sacrificing ease of play. Try one of ten new exciting scenarios! Designed for 5-6 players, it adds even more drama to the award-winning game of seafaring, exploration, and trade.

Belongs to the Catan Series. This game requires Catan, Catan 5/6 player extension, and Catan: Seafarers to play.  Ideally using the same publisher and edition of the game.

description: Players each try to be the first to build a complete snake, connecting the head and tail of the snake printed on their large start tiles.  On a player's turn he rolls a special die and plays one or more small tiles showing snake body parts to either his own snake or to his left-hand neighbor's snake.  How many tiles he plays and to which snake he plays the tiles depends on the roll of the special die.  The body tiles can contain turns, crossovers, and twists which can extend the distance your neighbor needs to cover to complete his snake.  But if the tile you select to play on your neighbor can't fit, it comes back to you!

Not a stats based game and not a "replay" game. Teams are fictional and can be built based on fixed points or amount of money the team has. Players are of three average, good, exceptional and cost different amounts of money to "hire". 
Money can either be a fixed amount in pick up games, or using the advanced economy rules, can be obtained from running concessions and selling broadcasting rights.

The dual-fold, multi-color game board folds for easy storage. Each comes with three teams of ten sculptured ABS figurines, each with individually stenciled numbers. A designated hitter is included on each team. Since the whole family can play BIG BUCKS BASEBALL, one set of figurines is female.

The game also includes 3 special challenging dice, 32 player upgrade snap rings, 12 ball/strike markers, 10 concession/broadcast replicas, 2 miniature game baseballs, 90 pieces of currency, 32 special event cards, 2 reproducible score sheets, and a complete instruction booklet and quick start guide. All of this is packaged in a compact 10-inch by 10-inch durable game box.

The game uses a unique system of roulette type spinner based on batter and pitcher's experience (3 levels) to determine the outcome of each pitch. Once a pitch is hit the defensive team can still field the ball using a system of color coded dice. Careful through, failing the fielding roll will increase the effectiveness of the hit (error). Also there are event cards such as injuries and automatic player upgrades (having a good night) which influence the game randomly.

The game consists of a playing board layout of a typical 18-hole championship golf course. A tee marker is moved along the course to indicate the number of strokes (rolls of the dice) a player has taken to "hole out." Twenty-five chips are given to each player at the start of the game, and these are given up as a player fails to keep their ball on track, eventually being won by whoever has the low score for the game. Also included are 3 dice and a pad of golf scorecards. The score is kept for each player as in golf.

53 Cards represent the 50 States, Washington DC and the states of Confusion and Ecstasy.

The object is to go out by laying down sets of 3 cards, where all the cards are adjacent states. The first person to rid their hand of all cards ends the round and points are scored. Play is to 500.

84-page handbook for Supremacy players.  Five sections:

1) Questions and Answers - FAQ for rules clarifications / errata
2) Rule Variants - Adds rules for convoys & escorts, multi-theater attacks, rocket technology, etc.
3) Strategy and Tactics
4) Rules for a Two-Player Game
5) Tournament Rules

Expands:

    Supremacy: The Game of the Superpowers

Players play a game of checkers with letter cubes.  Every time a move is made, the player can score points by forming words using the cube that was moved, and he can change the letter of that cube to help facilitate this.  The object of the game is to score the most points, and this occurs in two ways: forming words and winning the checkers match.

Each player draws a list of cities that they must visit, in sequence, which starts and ends at a particular city.  Players roll the dice and move along their chosen routes to reach their target cities.  Every time they throw doubles they must draw a card.  Most cards have a question about a state capitol that rewards the player for answering correctly, or punishes them for answering incorrectly.  Occasionally the cards have no question but just advance the player's car to the next destination.  The first player to reach all of their cities and return home is the winner.

Originally published 1890 (patent no. 469089), re-released by Parker 1901, 1910, 1922, 1952.

This game may well have been the inspiration for the later games Pirate and Traveler, Boxcars and Rail Baron.

description: "Connect Four" with a twist: the balls are kept hidden from both players, so they must try to remember which colored balls are where.  When a player thinks they have four in a row, they pull the lever that drops the balls into sight.  If they are right, they score a point.  If they are wrong, their opponent scores a point.  The first player to eight points wins.

boardgame: Man O' War: Sea of Blood
description:
From the box:

High above the waves, the crew of the Dwarf War Balloon watched aghast as the water churned around the Dreadnought far below. A Black Leviathan, all fangs and claws, erupted from the waves, bellowing in rage as it tore into the stricken vessel again and again.

Sea of Blood contains new rules to be used in conjunction with the Man O' War game and the Plague Fleet supplement.

SEA MONSTERS:
The Triton, Sea Elemental, Kraken, Sea Dragon, Megaladon, Promethean, Black Leviathan, Gargantuan and Behemoth rise to the surface to vent their fury against the fleets of the known world.

FLYERS:
Bretonnian Pegasus Riders, Dwarf Gyrocopters and War Balloons, Elf Dragon Riders and War Eagles, Imperial Griffon Riders, Orc Wyvern Riders, Dark Elf Manticore Lords, Chaos Dwarf Taurus Riders, and Lords of Change fill the smoke-laden skies above the battle.

Sea of Blood also contains rules for: Allies, Imperial Hell-hammers, Ironfists, Norse Raiders and Dwarf Dreadnoughts.

CONTENTS:

    1  x Rulebook
    9  x Sea Monster Templates
    14 x Flying Creature Templates

    18 x Anti-flier Armament
    33 x Norse Crew Counters

    3  x Ironfist Templates
    46 x Ship Templates


SEA OF BLOOD IS NOT A COMPLETE GAME. YOU WILL NEED A COPY OF THE MAN O' WAR GAME TO USE THE CONTENTS OF THIS BOX.

Shadow Wars is a fantasy strategy board game where each player chooses one of the four rulers within the game: Human, Dwarf, Elf or Dark Elf. These rulers have different advantages, disadvantages and play styles. Some rulers have mighty soldiers and defenses but are weaker in magic. Where as, other rulers are extremely powerful when it comes to magic but have weaker and fewer soldiers than the other rulers. Players must learn how to best use their ruler and play against the other three vastly different: Rulers, Units, Creatures, Magic, Buildings and Strategies. A full description and artwork can be found at: http://www.shadowwars.net

Each player starts with a Capital, two towns and one special building. From these locations the players can slowly build up their resources to obtain additional Towns, Cities and special buildings. Each new building will give that player an additional advantage, but also takes resources to build. A few examples of these buildings are: Armory, Mine, Mage Tower, Blood Forge, Dark Library & Cathedral. As each individual player expands their empire they can hire vast armies, summon powerful creatures & conquer new lands. With all of the various units and buildings each game is unique and, most of all, fun. The last player with a Capital left standing is the King of Syrrith!

Fire in Mississippi recreates the Civil War Battle of Corinth, October 3-4, 1862 when Confederate Generals Earl Van Dorn and Sterling Price hoped to seize the vital transportation center of Corinth, Mississippi.

Madame Guillotine is a multi-player simulation of the brutal power struggle taking place in Paris at the time of the French Revolution (aka The Terror), The players representing notable personalities of the time, distribute "influence points" with the aim of capturing the support of various sections of the society. While so doing, they must try and maneuver their opponents into untenable positions, deal with military and social crises, while still remaining on good terms with the populace.

The goal of the game is to try to set up a chain of domino-like pieces (starting with one of your own kings) that will topple one of the adversary's king pieces when tipped over. Each turn, you take two actions. The actions can be place a piece, turn a piece, move a piece (once all your pieces have been played), or topple a chain. If you knock down the opponent's king, it is out of the game. If you don't, you lose your king instead. Through your movement or rotation of pieces, you can interfere with your opponent's chain as well as build your own.
You spin an arrow and get to pick fence pieces which you use to enclose pig pens. If you enclose all your 4 pigs , you win.  Ages 5 and up................fun game to play with your children.

Victory in Europe is a historical game that simulates the collapse of Germany in World War II. The game is a corps/army level simulation of the climactic struggle to end the war in Germany.

400 counters

description:
In the 1920s, anthropologists, explorers and adventurers found a curious spiral-tracked game being played by the Baggara Arabs of the Sudan —Li'b el Merafib, “The Hyena Game”.  In all essential details the "Hyena Game" seems to have been identical to the Ancient Egyptian game of Mehen (The Game of the Coiled One).  The board's shape suggests it may be a distant ancestor of the Game of Goose but the mechanics are so different that this is probably a coincidence.

It was played on a spiraling track, employed stick dice (throwing sticks) of precisely the kind known from Archaic Egyptian contexts, and had two types of pieces, one representing a predatory animal.  The only difference would seem to be that the ancient Egyptians allotted six counters to each player rather than only one.  The Cretan Phaistos Disc may have been a Mehen board, but that remains speculation.

Mehen is the only multi-player ancient Egyptian board game known, the others (Senet, Aseb, Hounds & Jackals, etc.) being all two-player games.  The main function of the serpent-shaped Egyptian god Mehen was to protect the sun-god Ra from his enemies by coiling around him.  In the Old Kingdom, the race-game Mehen took the name and shape of the god: a coiled serpent with the gaming spaces on its back.  As "bodyguard", Mehen symbolises the sun-god's immediate neighborhood for the deceased.  The deceased walks towards Ra on the back of the serpent-god.

By the early Middle Kingdom, the ‘cuts’ used on the snake's back of the Mehen board to separate the playing spaces were thought to ‘kill’ the snake —which would have been a very threatening and evil thing.  Mehen’s role was essential, for if Ra were not protected from his enemies, he might not rise in the morning, which would result in the cessation of all life.  In Egyptian belief, ‘life’ applied not only to the living but also to the dead, who were believed to travel with the sun and to rise, reborn, with him at dawn.  From that time forth, the game apparently ceased to be played, possibly banned and forbidden.

boardgame: Black & White: The Adventures in Dairy Farming
description: Here's a game you don't see very often!  The object of this dairy farming game is to sell milk, increase your herd size, and build up your assets (money, herd builder values, and farm deed values).  The game is patterned after Milton Bradley's "The Game of Life".  At the start, players can go right into farming or choose instead to take out a loan and go to college to become a Veterinarian, Nutritionist, Geneticist or other professional.  Players buy farms and herds and make money by selling milk.  Along the way they are beset by various random events, hold auctions, switch herds with other players, and perform other actions along these lines.  The game ends when a player makes it to the end of the board.  The player with the most assets when the game ends is the winner.

boardgame: Wizard in Training Trading Card Game
description:
To those lucky enough to enter it's doors, the Dragontooth Academy opens up a whole new world - a world filled with magic spells, enchanted items, and warlike wraiths! Are you ready to become the single student worthy enough to graduate from the Academy as a full-fledged Wizard?

[ Object of the game ]
In Upper Deck's Wizard in Training card game, each player takes the role of a Dragontooth Academy student as they prepare for the climactic event of the school year - the graduation battle. Players use their wits and the resources available to them as they race to collect Dragonteeth in order to progress through 5 levels of Wizard proficiency. Along the way, they can cast spells against their opponents to steal Teeth and slow their progress. They can also choose to conjure a beast to battle and win Teeth for their cause. The higher the Wizard level, the more powerful the Spells and Duel Characters available to the players become. The first player to progress through all five Wizard levels graduates and wins the game!

[ "Create Your Own Wizard" Card ]
Each starter deck includes one "create your own Wizard" game card giving players the flexibility to create or bring their own favorite Wizard into play!

From the back:
The charm of Carmel-by-the-Sea, a storybook village of sun-dappled forests and jewel-like beaches has been captured by this exciting new board game.  What every visitor remembers fondly and residents cherish has been re-created on a 30” x 30” game board of Carmel streets and buildings nestled among the pines.  Players draw illustrated cards with the names of businesses where they must go to make specific purchases.  They map a direct course, roll the dice and drive through “Carmel Proper” to the businesses before continuing on to the site where the last gingerbread-style home, dubbed “Carmel Charmer” by the residents, will be auctioned off.  The highest bidder wins.  However, shopping in “Carmel Proper” is full of pitfalls.  No parking spaces can be found.  Fines are levied for double parking and pedestrians fill the streets, blocking the player’s route.  A chance card may send a player close to his goal or on a detour through the beautiful 17 Mile Drive.  A wary player who catches an opponent breaking any traffic rule receives a bonus of $1000 from the violator.  Car theft is $5000.

“Carmel Proper” is a game of distinction.  The board itself is an artistic rendering that depicts the tiny city with accuracy.  The movers are handcrafted wooden cars.  The money, beautifully printed on real parchment paper, increases the quality of the game…and exquisite addition to any home.

This is a Monopoly clone.  Instead of properties, players collect "preserves" to save animals.  Instead of houses and hotels, players buy environmental programs like a hunting ban or pollution control.  Chance and Community Chest cards are replaced by a single "action card" deck.  The mechanics of the games are the same, however: roll and move, if you land on someone else's preserve, pay them money.  If they have one or more programs on that preserve, pay them even more.  One difference is how the game ends: it can end either like Monopoly (last player left after everyone else goes bankrupt wins) or a player can collect five of the species for a particular geographical area (color) in which case they win.

From the back of the box:
ENDANGERED SPECIES is a game the entire family will enjoy.  It introduces players to the largest animal that ever lived, the fastest four-legged animal in the world, a huge bird of prey with a ten-foot wingspan, the most powerful ape, the fiercest carnivore in the Americas, and many other fascinating creatures now facing extinction.  Player try to “save” these animals by setting up “Preserves” to protect their natural habitats, and then establishing programs to protect them from hunting, capture for zoos, chemical pollution, and commercial exploitation.  To win, a player must save five species from one geographic area.  Unexpected gains and setbacks make the game unpredictable, and “action cards” held by each player permit a wide variety of strategic moves and counter moves.  No two games are ever the same.
Word game with 3 levels of difficulty.  Multiple choice questions with player challenge.  Contains 12,000 words and definitions.

From the back of the box:

Just think: you, a big network programming chief.  Buying shows and acquiring sponsors.  Arranging your own prime time programming schedule.  Knocking other shows out of the ratings.  Bargaining with the Union.  Winning Emmy Awards.  Winning the Good Broadcasting Award or getting zapped by the FCC.  That’s what you will experience when you play Prime Time, The Television Game.  Just as in real TV, the quality of the programming doesn’t matter much.  The object of this game is to make money.  Whoever has amassed the most profits at the end of the season is the winner.

boardgame: Henry VIII: Intrigue in the Tudor Court
description:
Free downloadable boardgame about intrigue in the court of Henry VIII from: http://www.garrysgames.com

Players represent factions of historical characters who scheme and plot to increase their influence. The winning Faction is the one with most Influence at the end of the Turn in which Henry dies.

A descendant of AH's old Down with the King, but with more streamlined game mechanics, and firmly rooted in the history of the Tudor court. Easily learned by non-gamers and a solid vehicle for teaching history as well as having fun. Cards are of professional quality and feature portraits of the historical figures involved.

Unlike other multi-player games, players don't just sit around waiting for their turn, but can influence play at any time.
